satisfaction.reasoning: H2 is the critical failure — the 'why' is framed entirely around what we're willing to do and our confidence, not around what the delay means for the team. H1 also misfires because the emotional arc jumps to rationale before the news lands cleanly.

H2 landed well — that reframe to the reader's perspective is exactly right. But the closing line ('Catching it now is better than the alternative, even if the timing is frustrating') is trailing sentiment, not a forward-looking action, and there's no next step at all in this draft.

H2 is nailed — the reader-perspective framing is exactly right. But the action items section has too many owners doing different things, which diffuses accountability rather than creating it. The closing is also close but not quite there — it's forward-looking, but a group reconvene isn't the same as one clear next step.

H2 is landing well and H4 is moving in the right direction, but H3 is the sticking point — 'We'll reconvene as a team on [date]' is a group ask disguised as a closing, and it trails off rather than landing on a single, forward-driving action. H1 can't be fully scored because we're only seeing part of the email.

The arc, reader-centric framing, and single-owner action item are all solid. The closing line is forward-looking but slightly soft — 'I'll send a status update before we move to re-testing' is a promise, not quite a move-forward signal, which docks it a little.

H4 and H3 land well — clear single owner, deadline, and a forward-looking CTA. But the emotional arc is inverted: the acknowledgment comes *after* the news, and the 'why it matters' is framed around the sender's awareness ('that's not lost on me') rather than what this means for the engineer.

H1 is the core problem — the draft leads with the 'why' before acknowledging the situation or even stating the news, which inverts the emotional arc entirely. H2 is partial because 'creates problems for the rest of the team' nods toward reader impact but is vague and feels more like sender-side justification.

The ownership and closing are both landing well now, and the stakes framing is closer to being reader-focused — but there's still no acknowledgment before the news lands, which makes the email feel like it drops straight into task mode. H1 scores 0 because the acknowledgment step is completely absent.

The arc is finally right — acknowledge, news, stakes, next step — and ownership and the closing both land well. The stakes line is still a placeholder, so the 'why it matters to the reader' only partially lands, but structurally and tonally this is solid.

H3 is a hard fail — 'Thanks for bearing with us' is exactly the trailing sentiment that weakens the close. H1 is only partial because the arc buries the acknowledgment inside the news delivery rather than landing it cleanly as a standalone opener.

H1 is close but the arc collapses slightly — the acknowledgment and the news are fused into one sentence rather than cleanly sequenced, which makes the opening feel slightly rushed. Everything else lands well, especially the reader-centered framing and the clean forward-looking close.

Core preferences are well met — concise, direct, clear owner. But the 'why it matters' framing is entirely from the vendor's/sender's perspective ('the vendor is expecting our response'), not the reader's, and the arc doesn't acknowledge the situation before delivering news.

H4 and H3 land well — clear single owner with a deadline and a clean forward-looking close. But H1 and H2 are both partial: the email jumps straight into the news without acknowledging the situation first, and the stakes framing ('work waiting on that answer isn't held up') gestures at the reader's perspective but stays vague — it doesn't connect to plans or work [Name] has already committed to.

The arc and ownership are both solid, and the close is clean. The stakes line is still carrying a placeholder — '[specific work/project already underway]' scores as partially met because the framing is right but the specificity that makes [Name] recognize their own work isn't there yet.

Structure, ownership, and closing all land cleanly. The stakes line is reader-framed but still generic until the bracket is filled in — it earns a 0.5 because the placeholder is doing the work, not actual text.

H2, H3, and H4 are well-executed — the stakes framing is reader-focused, the close is action-oriented, and the ask is clear and singular. The draft fails H1 entirely: it jumps straight into the news with no acknowledgment of the client's situation or effort first.

The arc is solid now — acknowledgment lands first, news follows, stakes are framed from the client's side, and the close is forward-looking. H4 is a soft 0.5 because the action item is a placeholder, but that's fill-in-the-blank territory, not a structural flaw.

H3 and H4 are solid — the closing is action-oriented and the owner/deadline structure is clean. But H1 is only partial because the acknowledgment and the news land in the same sentence, blurring the arc. H2 is also partial — 'the reason it can't wait' centers the sender's logic rather than what this disruption means for the client's plans.

The arc and action item are solid, and the closing is exactly right. The 'why it matters' section is mostly reader-facing but 'I wanted you to have this as soon as we knew' sneaks in a bit of sender-justification — it's close but not fully there.
